en.extract:
The South Island takahē (Porphyrio hochstetteri) is a flightless swamphen indigenous to New Zealand and the largest living member of the rail family. It is often known by the abbreviated name takahē, which it shares with the recently extinct North Island takahē. The two takahē species are also known as notornis.
Takahē were hunted extensively by both early European settlers and Māori, and takahē bones have been found in middens in the South Island. Fossil remains have also been found across the South Island. They were not named and described by Europeans until 1847, and then only from fossil bones. In 1850 a living bird was captured, and three more collected in the 19th century. After another bird was captured in 1898, and no more were to be found, the species was presumed extinct. Fifty years later, however, after a carefully planned search, South Island takahē were dramatically rediscovered in November 1948 by Geoffrey Orbell in an isolated valley in the South Island's Murchison Mountains. The species is now managed by the New Zealand Department of Conservation, whose Takahē Recovery Programme maintains populations on several offshore islands as well as Takahē Valley. Takahē has been reintroduced to numerous locations across the country. Although South Island takahē are still a threatened species, their NZTCS status was downgraded in 2016 from Nationally Critical to Nationally Vulnerable. As of 2023, the population is around 500 and is growing by 8 percent per year.


Scientific description and naming

Anatomist Richard Owen was sent fossil bird bones found in 1847 in South Taranaki on the North Island by collector Walter Mantell, and in 1848 he coined the genus Notornis ("southern bird") for them, naming the new species Notornis mantelli. The bird was presumed by Western science to be another extinct species like the moa.
Two years later, a group of sealers in Tamatea / Dusky Sound, Fiordland, encountered a large bird which they chased with their dogs. "It ran with great speed, and upon being captured uttered loud screams, and fought and struggled violently; it was kept alive three or four days on board the schooner and then killed, and the body roasted and ate by the crew, each partaking of the dainty, which was declared to be delicious." Walter Mantell happened to meet the sealers, and secured the bird's skin from them. He sent it to his father, palaeontologist Gideon Mantell, who realised this was Notornis, a living bird known only from fossil bones, and presented it in 1850 to a meeting of the Zoological Society of London. A second specimen was sent to Gideon Mantell in 1851, caught by Māori on Secretary Island, Fiordland. (Takahē were well known to Māori, who travelled long distances to hunt them. The bird's name comes from the Māori verb takahi, to stamp or trample.)
Only two more South Island takahē were collected by Europeans in the 19th century. One was caught by a rabbiter's dog on the eastern side of Lake Te Anau in 1879. It was bought by what is now the State Museum of Zoology, Dresden, for £105, and destroyed during the bombing of Dresden in World War II. Another takahē was caught by another dog, also on the shore of Lake Te Anau, on 7 August 1898; the dog, named 'Rough', was owned by musterer Jack Ross. Ross tried to revive the female takahē, but it died, and he delivered it to curator William Benham at Otago Museum. In excellent condition, it was purchased by the New Zealand government for £250 and was put on display; for many years it was the only mounted specimen in New Zealand, and the only takahē on display anywhere in the world.
After 1898, hunters and settlers continued to report sightings of large blue-and-green birds, described as "giant pukakis" (pūkeko or Australasian swamphens); one group chased but could not catch a bird "the size of a goose, with blue-green feathers and the speed of a racehorse". None of the sightings were authenticated, and the only specimens collected were fossil bones. The takahē was considered extinct.


Taxonomy and systematics

The third takahē collected went to the Königlich Zoologisches und Anthropologisch-Ethnographisches Museum in Dresden, and the Director Adolf Bernhard Meyer examined the skeleton while preparing his classification of the museum's birds, Abbildungen von Vogelskeletten (1879–1895). He decided the skeletal differences between the Fiordland bird and Owen's North Island specimen were sufficient to make it a separate species, which he called Notornis hochstetteri, after the Austrian geologist Ferdinand von Hochstetter.
Over the second half of the 20th century, the two Notornis species were gradually relegated to subspecies: Notornis mantelli mantelli in the North Island, and Notornis mantelli hochstetteri in the South. They were then incorporated into the same genus as the closely related Australasian swamphen or pūkeko (Porphyrio porphyrio), becoming a subspecies of Porphyrio mantelli. Pūkeko are members of a widespread species of swamphen, but based on fossil evidence have only been in New Zealand for a few hundred years, arriving from Australia after the islands were first settled by Polynesians.
A morphological and genetic study of living and extinct Porphyrio revealed that North and South Island takahē were, as originally proposed by Meyer, separate species. The North Island species (P. mantelli, as described by Owen) was known to Māori as moho; it is extinct and only known from skeletal remains and one possible specimen. Moho were taller and more slender than takahē, and share a common ancestor with living pūkeko. Although it was historically proposed that the two takahē species were unrelated, a genetic analysis published in 2024 suggested that they are each other's closest relatives and likely descended from a single ancestor that colonised New Zealand, with the split between the two species dated at around 4 to 1.5 million years ago.


Rediscovery
Living South Island takahē were rediscovered in an expedition led by Invercargill-based physician Geoffrey Orbell near Lake Te Anau in the Murchison Mountains, on 20 November 1948. The expedition started when footprints of an unknown bird were found near Lake Te Anau. Two takahē were caught but returned to the wild after photos were taken of the rediscovered bird.


Description

The South Island takahē is the largest living member of the family Rallidae. Its overall length averages 63 cm (25 in) and its average weight is about 2.7 kg (6.0 lb) in males and 2.3 kg (5.1 lb) in females, ranging from 1.8–4.2 kg (4.0–9.3 lb). The lifespan of a takahē can range from 18 years in the wild or 22 in animal sanctuaries. Its standing height is around 50 cm (20 in). It is a stocky, powerful bird, with short strong legs and a massive bill which can deliver a painful bite to the unwary. Although a flightless bird, the takahē sometimes uses its reduced wings to help it clamber up slopes.
South Island takahē plumage, beaks, and legs show typical gallinule colours. Adult takahē plumage is silky, iridescent, and mostly dark-blue or navy-blue on the head, neck, and underside, peacock blue on the wings. The back and inner wings are teal and green, becoming olive-green at the tail, which is white underneath. Takahē have a bright scarlet frontal shield and "carmine beaks marbled with shades of red". Their scarlet legs were described as "crayfish-red" by one of the early rediscoverers.
Sexes are similar; the females are slightly smaller, and may display frayed tail feathers when nesting. Chicks are covered with jet-black fluffy down when hatched, and have very large brown legs, with a dark white-tipped bill. Immature takahē have a duller version of adult colouring, with a dark bill that turns red as they mature.
South Island takahē are noisy. They have a non-directional warning womph call, which was described by the rediscoverers of takahē as someone "whistling to them over a .303 cartridge case", and a loud clowp call. The contact call is easily confused with that of the weka (Gallirallus australis), but is generally more resonant and deeper.


Behaviour and ecology

The South Island takahē is a sedentary and flightless bird currently found in alpine grasslands habitats. It is territorial and remains in the grassland until the arrival of snow, when it descends to the forest or scrub. It eats grass, shoots, and insects, but predominantly leaves of Chionochloa tussocks and other alpine grass species. The South Island takahē can often be seen plucking a snow grass (Danthonia flavescens) stalk, taking it into one claw, and eating only the soft lower parts, which appears to be its favourite food, while the rest is discarded.
A South Island takahē has been recorded feeding on a paradise duckling at Zealandia. Although this behaviour was previously unknown, the related Australasian swamphen or pūkeko occasionally feeds on eggs and nestlings of other birds as well.


Breeding
The South Island takahē is monogamous, with pairs remaining together from 12 years to, probably, their entire lives. It builds a bulky nest under bushes and scrub, and lays one to three buff eggs. The chick survival rate is between 25% and 80%, depending on location.


Distribution and habitat
The takahē's preferred habitat is alpine grasslands. The species is still present in the location where it was rediscovered in the Murchison Mountains. Small numbers have also been successfully translocated to five predator-free offshore islands, Tiritiri Matangi, Kapiti, Maud, Mana and Motutapu, where they can be viewed by the public. Additionally, captive takahē can be viewed at Te Anau and Pūkaha / Mount Bruce National wildlife centres. In June 2006 a pair of takahē were relocated to the Maungatautari Restoration Project. In September 2010 a pair of takahē (Hamilton and Guy) were released at Willowbank Wildlife Reserve – the first non-Department of Conservation institution to hold this species. In January 2011 two takahē were released in Zealandia, Wellington, and in mid-2015, two more takahē were released on Rotoroa Island in the Hauraki Gulf. There have also been relocations onto the Tawharanui Peninsula. In 2014 two pairs of Takahē were released into Wairakei golf and sanctuary, a private fenced sanctuary at Wairakei north of Taupō, the first chick was born there in November 2015.
At October 2017 there were 347 takahē accounted for, an increase of 41 over 2016. The Orokonui Ecosanctuary is home to a single takahē breeding pair, Quammen and Paku. The pair successfully bred two chicks in 2018, both of which died from exposure after heavy rains in November 2018. The deaths caused some controversy with regards to the Ecosanctuary's policy of "non-interference".
In 2018, eighteen South Island takahē were reintroduced to the Kahurangi National Park, 100 years after their local extinction.
Following the 2018 release, a second re-introduction has taken place on Te Waipounamu in August 2023, eighteen takahē were released in the Upper Whakatipu Waimāori Valley in Ngāi Tahu owned Greenstone Station. Later that year in October, six more takahē were released onto the property.


Status and conservation
The near extinction of the formerly widespread South Island takahē is due to a number of factors: over-hunting, loss of habitat and introduced predators have all played a part. The introduction of red deer (Cervus elaphus) represent a severe competition for food, while stoats (Mustela erminea) take a role as predators. The spread of the forests in post-glacial Pleistocene-Holocene has contributed to the reduction of habitat. Since the species is K-selected, i.e. is long-lived, reproduces slowly, takes several years to reach maturity, and had a large range that has drastically contracted in comparatively few generations, inbreeding depression is a significant problem. The recovery efforts are hampered especially by low fertility of the remaining birds. Genetic analyses have been employed to select captive breeding stock in an effort to preserve the maximum genetic diversity.


Decline of takahē
The causes of the pre-European decline of takahē were postulated by Williams (1962) and later supported in a detailed report by Mills et al. (1984). They held that climate changes were the main cause of the low numbers of takahē before European settlement. The environmental conditions prior to the period of European settlement were not suitable for takahē, and eliminated most of the population. The rising temperatures were not tolerated by this group of birds. Takahē are adapted to alpine grasslands, and the post-glacial era modified those zones, causing a sharp decline in the takahē population.
Secondly, they suggested that Polynesian settlers arriving about 800–1,000 years ago, bringing dogs and Polynesian rats (Rattus exulans) and hunting takahē for food, started another decline. European settlement in the nineteenth century almost wiped them out through hunting and introducing mammals such as deer which competed for food and predators (e.g. stoats) which preyed on them directly. 


Takahē population, conservation and protection

After long threats of extinction, South Island takahē now find protection in Fiordland National Park (New Zealand's largest national park). However, the species has not made a stable recovery in this habitat since they were rediscovered in November 1948. In fact, the takahē population was at 400 before it was reduced to 118 in 1982 due to competition with Fiordland domestic deer. Conservationists noticed the threat that deer posed to takahē survival, and the national park has now implemented deer control with hunting by helicopter.
The rediscovery of the South Island takahē caused great public interest. The New Zealand government took immediate action by closing off a remote part of Fiordland National Park to prevent the birds from being bothered. However, at the moment of rediscovery, there were different perspectives on how the bird should be conserved. At first, the Forest and Bird Society advocated for takahē to be left to work out their own "destiny", but many worried that the takahē would be incapable of making a comeback and thus become extinct like New Zealand's native huia. Interventionists then sought to relocate the takahē to "island sanctuaries" and breed them in captivity. Ultimately, no action was taken for nearly a decade due to a lack of resources and a desire to avoid conflict.
The Burwood Takahē Breeding Centre was opened in 1985 at a site near Te Anau. The initial approach was to incubate eggs collected from nests and raise them by hand. Staff used hand-held puppets that replayed sounds of adult contact calls while feeding and interacting with the chicks, to help prevent the birds becoming "imprinted" on humans. Fibreglass replicas of adult birds were also placed in areas where the chicks slept. These methods were not used after 2011.
Biologists from the Department of Conservation drew on their experience with designing remote island sanctuaries to establish a safe habitat for takahē and translocate birds onto Maud Island (Marlborough Sounds), Mana Island (near Wellington), Kapiti Island (Kapiti Coast), and Tiritiri Matangi Island (Hauraki Gulf). The success of these translocations has meant that the takahē's island metapopulation appears to have reached its carrying capacity, as revealed by the increasing ratio of non-breeding to breeding adults and declines in produced offspring. This may lead to reduced population growth rates and increased rates of inbreeding over time, thereby posing problems regarding the maintenance of genetic diversity and thus takahē survival in the long term.
Recently, human intervention has been required to maintain the breeding success of the takahē, which is relatively low in the wild compared to other, less threatened species, so methods such as the removal of infertile eggs from nests and the captive rearing of chicks have been introduced to manage the takahē population. The Fiordland takahē population has a successful degree of reproductive output due to these management methods: the number of chicks per pairing with infertile egg removal and captive rearing is 0.66, compared to 0.43 for regions without any breeding management.
It was reported that several takahē have accidentally been killed by hunters under contract to the Department of Conservation in the course of control measures aimed at reducing populations of the similar-looking pūkeko. One bird was killed in 2009 and four more—equivalent to 5% of the total population—in 2015.


Future efforts for protection

The original recovery strategies and goals set in the early 1980s, both long-term and short-term, are now well under way.
The programme to move South Island takahē to predator-free island refuges, where the birds also receive supplementary feeding, began in 1984. Takahē can now be found on five small islands; Maud Island in the Marlborough Sounds, Mana Island and Kapiti Island off Wellington's west coast, and Tiritiri Matangi Island and Motutapu Island in the Hauraki Gulf. The Department of Conservation also runs a captive breeding and rearing programme at the Burwood Breeding Centre near Te Anau which has up to 25 breeding pairs. Chicks are reared with minimal human contact. The offspring of the captive birds are used for new island releases and to add to the wild population in the Murchison Mountains. The Department of Conservation also manages wild takahē nests to boost the birds' recovery.
An important management development has been the stringent control of deer in the Murchison Mountains and other takahē areas of Fiordland National Park. Following the introduction of deer hunting by helicopter, deer numbers have decreased dramatically and alpine vegetation is now recovering from years of heavy browsing. This improvement in its habitat has helped to increase takahē breeding success and survival. As of 2009, ongoing research aims to measure the impact of attacks by stoats and thus decide whether stoats are a significant problem requiring management.


Population
One of the original long-term goals was to establish a self-sustaining population of well over 500 South Island takahē. The population stood at 263 at the beginning of 2013. In 2016 the population rose to 306 takahē. In 2017 the population rose to 347—a 13 percent increase from the last year. In 2019, it increased to 418. As of 2023, the population is around 500.


References


External links

Explore Species: South Island Takahe at eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
The Official Takahē Recovery Website

The '''South Island takahē''' ('''''Porphyrio hochstetteri''''') is a [[Flightless bird|flightless]] [[swamphen]] indigenous to [[New Zealand]] and the largest living member of the [[Rail (bird)|rail family]]. It is often known by the abbreviated name '''takahē''', which it shares with the [[Holocene extinction|recently extinct]] [[North Island takahē]]. The two takahē species are also known as '''notornis'''.

Takahē were hunted extensively by both early European settlers and [[Māori people|Māori]], and takahē bones have been found in middens in the South Island.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Boast |first=Alexander P. |date=2019 |title=A Holocene fossil South Island takahē (Porphyrio hochstetteri) in a high-altitude north-west Nelson cave |url=https://ndhadeliver.natlib.govt.nz/delivery/DeliveryManagerServlet?dps_pid=FL46442082 |journal=Notornis |volume=66 |pages=34–36 |access-date=20 January 2024 |archive-date=20 January 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240120004641/https://ndhadeliver.natlib.govt.nz/delivery/DeliveryManagerServlet?dps_pid=FL46442082 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Takahe turnaround |url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/national/blogs/7910809/Takahe-turnaround |date=2012-11-12 |access-date=2024-04-01 |work=[[Stuff (website)|Stuff]] |archive-date=1 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401032040/https://www.stuff.co.nz/national/blogs/7910809/Takahe-turnaround |url-status=live }}</ref> Fossil remains have also been found across the South Island.<ref name=":1" /> They were not named and described by Europeans until 1847, and then only from fossil bones. In 1850 a living bird was captured, and three more collected in the 19th century. After another bird was captured in 1898, and no more were to be found, the species was presumed extinct. Fifty years later, however, after a carefully planned search, South Island takahē were dramatically rediscovered in November 1948 by [[Geoffrey Orbell]] in an isolated valley in the [[South Island]]'s [[Murchison Mountains]]. The species is now managed by the New Zealand [[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]], whose Takahē Recovery Programme maintains populations on several offshore islands as well as Takahē Valley. Takahē has been reintroduced to numerous locations across the country. Although South Island takahē are still a threatened species, their [[New Zealand Threat Classification System|NZTCS]] status was downgraded in 2016 from Nationally Critical to Nationally Vulnerable.<ref name="NZTCS" /> As of 2023, the population is around 500 and is growing by 8 percent per year.<ref name="McClure2023"/>

==Scientific description and naming==
[[File:Notornis Mantelli Owen (cropped).png|thumb|The first illustration of the South Island takahē from [[Gideon Mantell|Gideon]] and [[Walter Mantell]]'s notice of the discovery in 1850]]

Anatomist [[Richard Owen]] was sent fossil bird bones found in 1847 in [[South Taranaki District|South Taranaki]] on the [[North Island]] by collector [[Walter Mantell]], and in 1848 he coined the genus ''Notornis'' ("southern bird") for them, naming the new species ''Notornis mantelli''.<ref>Owen, R. (1848). "On ''Dinornis'' (Part III): containing a description of the skull and beak of that genus, and of the same characteristic parts of ''Palapteryx'', and of two other genera of birds, ''Notornis'' and ''Nestor'', forming part of an extensive collection of ornithic remains discovered by Mr Walter Mantell at Waingongoro, North Island of New Zealand". ''Transactions of the Zoological Society of London'' '''3''': 345–378, pls 52–56.</ref> The bird was presumed by Western science to be another extinct species like the [[moa]].

Two years later, a group of [[Seal hunting|sealers]] in [[Tamatea / Dusky Sound]], [[Fiordland]], encountered a large bird which they chased with their dogs. "It ran with great speed, and upon being captured uttered loud screams, and fought and struggled violently; it was kept alive three or four days on board the schooner and then killed, and the body roasted and ate by the crew, each partaking of the dainty, which was declared to be delicious."<ref name=":0" /> Walter Mantell happened to meet the sealers, and secured the bird's skin from them. He sent it to his father, palaeontologist [[Gideon Mantell]], who realised this was ''Notornis'', a living bird known only from fossil bones, and presented it in 1850 to a meeting of the [[Zoological Society of London]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1111/j.1469-7998.1862.tb08048.x|title = V. Notice of the Discovery by Mr. Walter Mantellin the Middle Island of New Zealand, of a Living Specimen of the Notornis, a Bird of the Rail Family, allied to Brachypteryx, and hitherto unknown to Naturalists except in a Fossil State|journal = The Transactions of the Zoological Society of London|volume = 4|issue = 2|pages = 69–72|year = 2010|last1 = Mantell|first1 = Gideon Algernon}}</ref> A second specimen was sent to Gideon Mantell in 1851, caught by [[Māori people|Māori]] on [[Secretary Island]], Fiordland. (Takahē were well known to Māori, who travelled long distances to hunt them. The bird's name comes from the [[Māori language|Māori]] verb ''takahi'', to stamp or trample.<ref name="Ballance2001">{{Cite book|title=The Takahe: Fifty Years of Conservation Management and Research|last=Ballance|first=Alison A.|publisher=University of Otago Press|year=2001|isbn=978-1-877276-01-9|editor-last=Lee|editor-first=William G.|location=Dunedin, New Zealand|pages=18–22|chapter=Takahe: the bird that twice came back from the grave|editor-last2=Jamieson|editor-first2=Ian G.}}</ref>)

Only two more South Island takahē were collected by Europeans in the 19th century. One was caught by a rabbiter's dog on the eastern side of [[Lake Te Anau]] in 1879. It was bought by what is now the [[State Museum of Zoology, Dresden]], for £105, and destroyed during the [[bombing of Dresden in World War II]].<ref name="Ballance2001" /> Another takahē was caught by another dog, also on the shore of Lake Te Anau, on 7 August 1898; the dog, named 'Rough', was owned by musterer Jack Ross. Ross tried to revive the female takahē, but it died, and he delivered it to curator [[William Benham (zoologist)|William Benham]] at [[Otago Museum]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.odt.co.nz/lifestyle/magazine/rare-all-its-forms|title=Rare in all its forms|last=Sima|first=Ellen|date=10 November 2014|work=[[Otago Daily Times]] |access-date=5 December 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url= https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov/lccn/sn85066387/1898-10-09/ed-1/seq-2/print/image_681x648_from_2343%2C3864_to_4101%2C5538/
 |title= Rare Bird Caught by Tourist's Dog| author= The San Francisco Call|date= October 21, 1898|website= Chronicling America|publisher=The San Francisco Call|access-date= July 21, 2022}}</ref> In excellent condition, it was purchased by the New Zealand government for £250 and was put on display; for many years it was the only mounted specimen in New Zealand, and the only takahē on display anywhere in the world.<ref name="Ballance2001" />

After 1898, hunters and settlers continued to report sightings of large blue-and-green birds, described as "giant pukakis" (pūkeko or [[Australasian swamphen]]s); one group chased but could not catch a bird "the size of a goose, with blue-green feathers and the speed of a racehorse".<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Grzelewski|first=Derek|date=Jan–Mar 1999|title=Takahe – the bird that came back from the dead|url=https://www.nzgeo.com/stories/takahe-the-bird-that-came-back-from-the-dead/|journal=New Zealand Geographic|volume=41|access-date=5 December 2018|archive-date=5 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181205105157/https://www.nzgeo.com/stories/takahe-the-bird-that-came-back-from-the-dead/|url-status=live}}</ref> None of the sightings were authenticated, and the only specimens collected were fossil bones.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gurr|first=L.|date=1952|title=A Skeleton of ''Notornis hochstetteri'' Meyer from Waitati, Otago, and Notes on Distribution of the Bird in the South Island, N.Z.|url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/TPRSNZ1952-80.2.17.2|journal=Transactions and Proceedings of the Royal Society of New Zealand|volume=80|issue=1|pages=19–21|access-date=5 December 2018|archive-date=5 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181205103421/https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/TPRSNZ1952-80.2.17.2|url-status=live}}</ref> The takahē was considered extinct.<ref name="Ballance2001" />

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
[[File:Notornis hochstetteri 34.jpg|thumb|''Notornis hochstetteri'', from Meyer's 1883 description]]
The third takahē collected went to the Königlich Zoologisches und Anthropologisch-Ethnographisches Museum in [[Dresden]], and the Director [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer]] examined the skeleton<ref>{{cite news |author1=B.Magee |title=New Zealand's Mystery Bird |journal=[[Otago Daily Times]] |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19380115.2.18 |access-date=18 May 2019 |agency=[[Otago Daily Times]] |issue=23400 |publisher=Allied Press |date=15 January 1938 |page=5 |issn=0114-426X |language=en |format=Broadsheet |quote=Only, four specimens of: the Notornis are preserved. Two are in the British Museum, another is in the Dresden Museum in Germany |archive-date=18 May 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190518033754/https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19380115.2.18 |url-status=live }}</ref> while preparing his classification of the museum's birds, ''Abbildungen von Vogelskeletten'' (1879–1895). He decided the skeletal differences between the Fiordland bird and Owen's North Island specimen were sufficient to make it a separate species, which he called ''Notornis hochstetteri'', after the Austrian geologist [[Ferdinand von Hochstetter]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/51853|title=Abbildungen von Vogel-Skeletten|last=Meyer|first=Adolf Bernhard|date=1883|location=Berlin|pages=28–30|doi=10.5962/bhl.title.51853|hdl=2027/chi.096066971|access-date=5 December 2018|archive-date=5 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181205145732/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/51853|url-status=live}}</ref>

Over the second half of the 20th century, the two ''Notornis'' species were gradually relegated to subspecies: ''Notornis mantelli mantelli'' in the North Island, and ''Notornis mantelli hochstetteri'' in the South. They were then incorporated into the same genus as the closely related [[Australasian swamphen]] or pūkeko (''Porphyrio porphyrio''), becoming a subspecies of ''Porphyrio mantelli''.<ref name="Trewick2001" /> Pūkeko are members of a widespread species of swamphen, but based on fossil evidence have only been in New Zealand for a few hundred years, arriving from Australia after the islands were first settled by [[Polynesians]].<ref name="Trewick2001" />

A morphological and genetic study of living and extinct ''Porphyrio'' revealed that North and South Island takahē were, as originally proposed by Meyer, separate species.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Trewick|first=Steven A.|date=1996|title=Morphology and evolution of two takahe: flightless rails of New Zealand|journal=Journal of Zoology|volume=238|issue=2|pages=221–237|doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.1996.tb05391.x}}</ref> The [[North Island takahē|North Island species]] (''P. mantelli'', as described by Owen) was known to Māori as ''moho''; it is extinct and only known from skeletal remains and one possible specimen. Moho were taller and more slender than takahē, and share a common ancestor with living pūkeko.<ref name="Trewick2001">{{Cite book|title=The Takahe: Fifty Years of Conservation Management and Research|last1=Trewick|first1=Steven A.|last2=Worthy|first2=Trevor H.|publisher=University of Otago Press|year=2001|isbn=978-1-877276-01-9|editor-last=Lee|editor-first=William G.|location=Dunedin, New Zealand|pages=31–48|chapter=Origins and prehistoric ecology of takahe based on morphometric, molecular, and fossil data|editor-last2=Jamieson|editor-first2=Ian G.}}</ref> Although it was historically proposed that the two takahē species were unrelated, a genetic analysis published in 2024 suggested that they are each other's closest relatives and likely descended from a single ancestor that colonised New Zealand, with the split between the two species dated at around 4 to 1.5 million years ago.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Verry |first1=Alexander J. F. |last2=Mas-Carrió |first2=Eduard |last3=Gibb |first3=Gillian C. |last4=Dutoit |first4=Ludovic |last5=Robertson |first5=Bruce C. |last6=Waters |first6=Jonathan M. |last7=Rawlence |first7=Nicolas J. |date=February 2024 |title=Ancient mitochondrial genomes unveil the origins and evolutionary history of New Zealand's enigmatic takahē and moho |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mec.17227 |journal=Molecular Ecology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=3 |article-number=e17227 |doi=10.1111/mec.17227 |pmid=38018770 |issn=0962-1083|doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Rediscovery ==
Living South Island takahē were rediscovered in an expedition led by [[Invercargill]]-based physician [[Geoffrey Orbell]] near [[Lake Te Anau]] in the [[Murchison Mountains]], on 20 November 1948.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2016-09-28 |title=Takahē – back from the brink |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/national/programmes/ourchangingworld/audio/201817903/takahe-back-from-the-brink |access-date=2024-04-29 |website=[[RNZ]] |language=en-nz |archive-date=29 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240429051907/https://www.rnz.co.nz/national/programmes/ourchangingworld/audio/201817903/takahe-back-from-the-brink |url-status=live }}</ref> The expedition started when footprints of an unknown bird were found near [[Lake Te Anau]]. Two takahē were caught but returned to the wild after photos were taken of the rediscovered bird.<ref>{{cite news |title=Gained in Telling |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19490108.2.76 |work=[[Otago Daily Times]] |via=Papers Past |access-date=7 April 2019 |page=6 |language=en |date=8 January 1949 |archive-date=25 March 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190325054830/https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19490108.2.76 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":2" />

==Description==
[[File:Female and male takahe standing in the grass.jpg|thumb|The colour of both female and male adults is mainly purple-blue with a greenish back and inner wings.]]
[[File:Two juvenile and two adult takahe standing on grass.jpg|thumb|Two juveniles and two adults (with red beaks) at [[Orokonui Ecosanctuary]]]]
The South Island takahē is the largest living member of the family [[Rallidae]]. Its overall length averages {{convert|63|cm|in|abbr=on}} and its average weight is about {{convert|2.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|2.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in females, ranging from {{convert|1.8|-|4.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. The [[Life expectancy|lifespan]] of a takahē can range from 18 years in the wild or 22 in [[Animal sanctuary|animal sanctuaries]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/nature/native-animals/birds/birds-a-z/takahe/|title=Takahē|access-date=23 January 2022|archive-date=28 March 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230328162945/https://www.doc.govt.nz/nature/native-animals/birds/birds-a-z/takahe/|url-status=live}}</ref> Its standing height is around {{convert|50|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name="LandJIntro2001">{{Cite book|title=The Takahe: Fifty Years of Conservation Management and Research|last1=Lee|first1=William G.|last2=Jamieson|first2=Ian G.|publisher=University of Otago Press|year=2001|isbn=978-1-877276-01-9|editor-last=Lee|editor-first=William G.|location=Dunedin, New Zealand|pages=11–17|chapter=Introduction|editor-last2=Jamieson|editor-first2=Ian G.}}</ref> It is a stocky, powerful bird, with short strong legs and a massive bill which can deliver a painful bite to the unwary. Although a flightless bird, the takahē sometimes uses its reduced wings to help it clamber up slopes.<ref name="LandJIntro2001" />

South Island takahē plumage, beaks, and legs show typical [[Moorhen|gallinule]] colours. Adult takahē plumage is silky, [[Iridescence|iridescent]], and mostly dark-blue or navy-blue on the head, neck, and underside, peacock blue on the wings. The back and inner wings are teal and green, becoming olive-green at the tail, which is white underneath.<ref name="LandJIntro2001" /> Takahē have a bright scarlet [[frontal shield]] and "carmine beaks marbled with shades of red".<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Grzelewski|first=Derek|date=May–Jun 2012|title=Great experimentations|url=https://www.nzgeo.com/stories/great-experimentations/|journal=New Zealand Geographic|volume=115}}</ref> Their scarlet legs were described as "crayfish-red" by one of the early rediscoverers.<ref name="Telfer2001">{{Cite book|title=The Takahe: Fifty Years of Conservation Management and Research|last=Watson (née Telfer)|first=Joan L.|publisher=University of Otago Press|year=2001|isbn=978-1-877276-01-9|editor-last=Lee|editor-first=William G.|location=Dunedin, New Zealand|pages=23–30|chapter=Notornis Rediviva|editor-last2=Jamieson|editor-first2=Ian G.}}</ref>

Sexes are similar; the females are slightly smaller, and may display frayed tail feathers when nesting.<ref name="Telfer2001" /> Chicks are covered with jet-black fluffy down when hatched, and have very large brown legs, with a dark white-tipped bill.<ref name="Telfer2001" /> Immature takahē have a duller version of adult colouring, with a dark bill that turns red as they mature.

South Island takahē are noisy. They have a non-directional warning ''{{not a typo|womph}}'' call, which was described by the rediscoverers of takahē as someone "whistling to them over a [[.303 British|.303]] cartridge case",<ref name="Telfer2001" /> and a loud ''{{not a typo|clowp}}'' call. The contact call is easily confused with that of the [[weka]] (''Gallirallus australis''), but is generally more resonant and deeper.<ref>del Hoyo, J. Elliott, A. & Sargatal, J. (editors). (1996) ''Handbook of the Birds of the World. Volume 3: Hoatzin to Auks''. Lynx Edicions. {{ISBN|84-87334-20-2}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Takahe parent and child 2.jpg|thumb|Adult feeding a chick]]
The South Island takahē is a sedentary and flightless bird currently found in alpine grasslands habitats. It is territorial and remains in the grassland until the arrival of snow, when it descends to the forest or scrub. It eats grass, shoots, and insects, but predominantly leaves of ''[[Chionochloa]]'' tussocks and other alpine grass species.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Mills|first1=J. A.|last2=Mark|first2=A. F.|date=1977|title=Food Preferences of Takahe in Fiordland National Park, New Zealand, and the Effect of Competition from Introduced Red Deer|journal=Journal of Animal Ecology|volume=46|issue=3|pages=939–958|doi=10.2307/3651|jstor=3651|bibcode=1977JAnEc..46..939M }}</ref> The South Island takahē can often be seen plucking a snow grass (''Danthonia flavescens'') stalk, taking it into one claw, and eating only the soft lower parts, which appears to be its favourite food, while the rest is discarded.

A South Island takahē has been recorded feeding on a [[paradise shelduck|paradise duckling]] at [[Zealandia (wildlife sanctuary)|Zealandia]]. Although this behaviour was previously unknown, the related [[Australasian swamphen]] or pūkeko occasionally feeds on eggs and nestlings of other birds as well.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/national/6062733/Its-a-bird-eat-bird-world| title=It's a bird eat bird world| work=[[Stuff (website)|Stuff]] | date=30 November 2011| first=Paul| last=Easton| access-date=7 June 2021| archive-date=6 June 2021| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210606213514/https://www.stuff.co.nz/national/6062733/Its-a-bird-eat-bird-world| url-status=live}}</ref>

===Breeding===
The South Island takahē is monogamous, with pairs remaining together from 12 years to, probably, their entire lives. It builds a bulky nest under bushes and scrub, and lays one to three buff [[egg]]s. The chick survival rate is between 25% and 80%, depending on location.<ref>{{cite web |title=Takahē and the Takahē Recovery Programme Fact Sheet, 2018–2019 |url=https://static.sciencelearn.org.nz/documents/files/000/000/754/original/Takah%C4%93_and_the_Takah%C4%93_Recovery_Programme_Fact_Sheet.pdf?1549353275 |publisher=Department of Conservation |access-date=4 July 2020 |page=1 |date=2019 |archive-date=6 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200706085721/https://static.sciencelearn.org.nz/documents/files/000/000/754/original/Takah%C4%93_and_the_Takah%C4%93_Recovery_Programme_Fact_Sheet.pdf?1549353275 |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The takahē's preferred habitat is alpine grasslands.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mills |first=J. A. |last2=Lavers |first2=R. B. |last3=Lee |first3=W. G. |date=1984 |title=The Takahe — a Relict of the Pleistocene Grassland Avifauna of New Zealand |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/24052703 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=7 |pages=57–70 |issn=0110-6465}}</ref> The species is still present in the location where it was rediscovered in the Murchison Mountains. Small numbers have also been successfully translocated to five predator-free offshore islands, [[Tiritiri Matangi]], [[Kapiti Island|Kapiti]], [[Maud Island|Maud]], [[Mana Island, New Zealand|Mana]] and [[Motutapu Island|Motutapu]], where they can be viewed by the public. Additionally, captive takahē can be viewed at [[Te Anau]] and [[Pūkaha / Mount Bruce National Wildlife Centre|Pūkaha / Mount Bruce National]] wildlife centres. In June 2006 a pair of takahē were relocated to the [[Maungatautari Restoration Project]]. In September 2010 a pair of takahē (Hamilton and Guy) were released at [[Willowbank Wildlife Reserve]] – the first non-Department of Conservation institution to hold this species. In January 2011 two takahē were released in [[Zealandia (wildlife sanctuary)|Zealandia, Wellington]], and in mid-2015, two more takahē were released on [[Rotoroa Island]] in the [[Hauraki Gulf]]. There have also been relocations onto the Tawharanui Peninsula. In 2014 two pairs of Takahē were released into Wairakei golf and sanctuary, a private fenced sanctuary at Wairakei north of Taupō, the first chick was born there in November 2015.
At October 2017 there were 347 takahē accounted for, an increase of 41 over 2016.<ref name="auto">{{Cite web|url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2017/takahe-population-100-breeding-pairs-strong/|title=Takahē population 100 breeding pairs strong|website=[[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]] |access-date=10 March 2019|archive-date=26 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190126124845/https://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2017/takahe-population-100-breeding-pairs-strong/|url-status=live}}</ref> The [[Orokonui Ecosanctuary]] is home to a single takahē breeding pair, Quammen and Paku. The pair successfully bred two chicks in 2018, both of which died from exposure after heavy rains in November 2018.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.odt.co.nz/news/dunedin/orokonui-takahe-chicks-victims-flood|title=Orokonui takahe chicks victims of flood|first=Jono|last=Edwards|date=27 November 2018|website=[[Otago Daily Times]] |access-date=3 March 2019|archive-date=6 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190306043826/https://www.odt.co.nz/news/dunedin/orokonui-takahe-chicks-victims-flood|url-status=live}}</ref> The deaths caused some controversy with regards to the Ecosanctuary's policy of "non-interference".<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=12168808|title=Department of Conservation blames 'bad parenting' for deaths of takahe chicks|first=Jono|last=Edwards|date=29 November 2018|via=www.nzherald.co.nz|access-date=3 March 2019|archive-date=6 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190306042952/https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=12168808|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2018, eighteen South Island takahē were reintroduced to the [[Kahurangi National Park]], 100 years after their local extinction.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/environment/102423974/first-population-of-takah-outside-of-fiordland-released-into-wild|title=First population of takahē outside of Fiordland released into wild|website=[[Stuff (website)|Stuff]] |date=20 March 2018|access-date=26 October 2019|archive-date=6 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190506212125/https://www.stuff.co.nz/environment/102423974/first-population-of-takah-outside-of-fiordland-released-into-wild|url-status=live}}</ref>

Following the 2018 release, a second re-introduction has taken place on [[South Island|Te Waipounamu]] in August 2023, eighteen takahē were released in the Upper [[Lake Wakatipu|Whakatipu Waimāori]] Valley in [[Ngāi Tahu]] owned [[Greenstone and Caples Tracks|Greenstone Station]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=August 25, 2023 |title=New Wild Home For Takahē On Ngāi Tahu Whenua In Ōtākou/Otago |url=https://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/AK2308/S00417/new-wild-home-for-takahe-on-ngai-tahu-whenua-in-otakouotago.htm |access-date=2023-08-29 |publisher=Department of Conservation |website=www.scoop.co.nz |archive-date=24 August 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230824222022/https://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/AK2308/S00417/new-wild-home-for-takahe-on-ngai-tahu-whenua-in-otakouotago.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> Later that year in October, six more takahē were released onto the property.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Takahē breeding success – five nests in Greenstone Valley |url=https://crux.org.nz/crux-news/takahe-breeding-success-5-nests-in-greenstone-valley |last=Pattemore |first=Lauren |date=2023-11-17 |access-date=2024-04-01 |work=Crux |archive-date=1 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401030350/https://crux.org.nz/crux-news/takahe-breeding-success-5-nests-in-greenstone-valley |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Status and conservation==
The near extinction of the formerly widespread South Island takahē is due to a number of factors: over-hunting, loss of habitat and introduced predators have all played a part. The introduction of [[red deer]] (''Cervus elaphus'') represent a severe competition for food, while [[stoat]]s (''Mustela erminea'') take a role as predators. The spread of the forests in post-glacial [[Pleistocene]]-[[Holocene]] has contributed to the reduction of habitat. Since the species is [[K-select]]ed, i.e. is long-lived, reproduces slowly, takes several years to reach maturity, and had a large range that has drastically contracted in comparatively few generations, [[inbreeding depression]] is a significant problem.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Grueber|first1=Catherine E.|last2=Laws|first2=Rebecca J.|last3=Nakagawa |first3=Shinichi |last4=Jamieson |first4=Ian G.|date=2010-11-11|title=Inbreeding Depression Accumulation across Life-History Stages of the Endangered Takahe|journal=Conservation Biology|language=en|volume=24|issue=6|pages=1617–1625|doi=10.1111/j.1523-1739.2010.01549.x|pmid=20586788|bibcode=2010ConBi..24.1617G |s2cid=19498813|issn=0888-8892}}</ref> The recovery efforts are hampered especially by low fertility of the remaining birds. Genetic analyses have been employed to select captive breeding stock in an effort to preserve the maximum genetic diversity.<ref>BirdLife International 2009. Species factsheet: [http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=2929&m=0 Porphyrio hochstetteri]. Retrieved 1 December 2009.</ref>

===Decline of takahē===
The causes of the pre-European decline of takahē were postulated by Williams (1962) and later supported in a detailed report by Mills ''et al.'' (1984).<ref>Williams, G.R. (1962) The Takahe (''Notornis mantelli'' Owen 1848); a general survey. ''Trans. Royal Soc. New Zealand'' 88:235–258.</ref><ref>Mills, J.A. Lavers, R.B. & Lee, W.G. (1984) The Takahe: A relict of the Pleistocene grassland avifauna of New Zealand. ''New Zealand Journal of Ecology'' 7:57–70.</ref> They held that climate changes were the main cause of the low numbers of takahē before European settlement. The environmental conditions prior to the period of European settlement were not suitable for takahē, and eliminated most of the population. The rising temperatures were not tolerated by this group of birds. Takahē are adapted to alpine grasslands, and the post-glacial era modified those zones, causing a sharp decline in the takahē population.{{citation needed|date=December 2023}}

Secondly, they suggested that Polynesian settlers arriving about 800–1,000 years ago, bringing dogs and [[Polynesian rat]]s (''Rattus exulans'') and hunting takahē for food, started another decline. European settlement in the nineteenth century almost wiped them out through hunting and introducing mammals such as deer which competed for food and predators (e.g. stoats) which preyed on them directly. {{citation needed|date=October 2012}}

===Takahē population, conservation and protection===
[[File:Takahe noa.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ringed female South Island takahē at [[Kapiti Island]]]]
After long threats of extinction, South Island takahē now find protection in [[Fiordland National Park]] (New Zealand's largest national park). However, the species has not made a stable recovery in this habitat since they were rediscovered in November 1948.<ref>{{Cite encyclopedia |title=Southland region – Plants and animals |encyclopedia=Te Ara – the Encyclopedia of New Zealand |last=Grant |first=David |url=http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/photograph/20091/takahe-rediscovered |access-date=28 March 2024 |date=1 May 2015 |archive-date=15 May 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240515130501/https://teara.govt.nz/en/photograph/20091/takahe-rediscovered |url-status=live }}</ref> In fact, the takahē population was at 400 before it was reduced to 118 in 1982 due to competition with [[Fiordland]] domestic deer. Conservationists noticed the threat that deer posed to takahē survival, and the national park has now implemented deer control with hunting by helicopter.<ref name="RecoveryPlan">{{Cite Q|Q110612077}}</ref>

The rediscovery of the South Island takahē caused great public interest. The New Zealand government took immediate action by closing off a remote part of [[Fiordland National Park]] to prevent the birds from being bothered. However, at the moment of rediscovery, there were different perspectives on how the bird should be conserved. At first, the Forest and Bird Society advocated for takahē to be left to work out their own "destiny",{{Citation needed|date=February 2018}} but many worried that the takahē would be incapable of making a comeback and thus become extinct like New Zealand's native [[huia]]. Interventionists then sought to relocate the takahē to "island sanctuaries" and breed them in captivity. Ultimately, no action was taken for nearly a decade due to a lack of resources and a desire to avoid conflict.{{citation needed|date=April 2020}}

The Burwood Takahē Breeding Centre was opened in 1985 at a site near Te Anau.<ref>{{Cite news |title=New Wakatipu home a 'last throw of the dice' for struggling takahē |url=https://www.thepress.co.nz/environment/350104742/new-wakatipu-home-last-throw-dice-struggling-takahe |last=Jamieson |first=Debbie |date=2023-11-05 |access-date=2024-04-01 |work=[[The Press]] |archive-date=1 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401032856/https://www.thepress.co.nz/environment/350104742/new-wakatipu-home-last-throw-dice-struggling-takahe |url-status=live }}</ref> The initial approach was to incubate eggs collected from nests and raise them by hand. Staff used hand-held puppets that replayed sounds of adult contact calls while feeding and interacting with the chicks, to help prevent the birds becoming "imprinted" on humans. Fibreglass replicas of adult birds were also placed in areas where the chicks slept. These methods were not used after 2011.<ref name="hub">{{cite web| url=https://www.sciencelearn.org.nz/resources/2704-takahe-conservation-efforts| title=Takahē conservation efforts| publisher=Science Learning Hub – Pokapū Akoranga Pūtaiao| access-date=7 June 2021| archive-date=6 June 2021| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210606222530/https://www.sciencelearn.org.nz/resources/2704-takahe-conservation-efforts| url-status=live}}</ref>

Biologists from the Department of Conservation drew on their experience with designing remote island sanctuaries to establish a safe habitat for takahē and translocate birds onto [[Maud Island]] (Marlborough Sounds), [[Mana Island (New Zealand)|Mana Island]] (near Wellington), [[Kapiti Island]] (Kapiti Coast), and [[Tiritiri Matangi Island]] (Hauraki Gulf). The success of these translocations has meant that the takahē's island metapopulation appears to have reached its carrying capacity, as revealed by the increasing ratio of non-breeding to breeding adults and declines in produced offspring. This may lead to reduced population growth rates and increased rates of inbreeding over time, thereby posing problems regarding the maintenance of genetic diversity and thus takahē survival in the long term.{{citation needed|date=April 2020}}

Recently, human intervention has been required to maintain the breeding success of the takahē, which is relatively low in the wild compared to other, less threatened species, so methods such as the removal of infertile eggs from nests and the captive rearing of chicks have been introduced to manage the takahē population. The Fiordland takahē population has a successful degree of reproductive output due to these management methods: the number of chicks per pairing with infertile egg removal and captive rearing is 0.66, compared to 0.43 for regions without any breeding management.{{citation needed|date=April 2020}}

It was reported that several takahē have accidentally been killed by hunters under contract to the Department of Conservation in the course of control measures aimed at reducing populations of the similar-looking [[pūkeko]]. One bird was killed in 2009 and four more—equivalent to 5% of the total population—in 2015.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/the-press/news/national/519560/Takahe-shot-in-case-of-mistaken-identity|title=Takahe shot in case of mistaken identity|date=31 January 2009|work=[[Stuff (website)|Stuff]] |access-date=20 December 2017|archive-date=22 December 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171222060848/http://www.stuff.co.nz/the-press/news/national/519560/Takahe-shot-in-case-of-mistaken-identity|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2015/aug/21/new-zealand-conservationists-apologise-over-accidental-shooting-of-endangered-takahe|title=New Zealand hunters apologise over accidental shooting of takahē|date=21 August 2015|author=Elle Hunt|work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=20 December 2017|archive-date=15 July 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715101407/https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2015/aug/21/new-zealand-conservationists-apologise-over-accidental-shooting-of-endangered-takahe|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Future efforts for protection===
[[File:TakaheMaunga.jpg|thumb|South Island takahē released at [[Maungatautari Restoration Project]] [[ecological island]], [[Waikato District]], North Island in June 2006]]
The original recovery strategies and goals set in the early 1980s, both long-term and short-term, are now well under way.

The programme to move South Island takahē to predator-free island refuges, where the birds also receive supplementary feeding, began in 1984. Takahē can now be found on five small islands; [[Maud Island]] in the Marlborough Sounds, [[Mana Island (New Zealand)|Mana Island]] and [[Kapiti Island]] off Wellington's west coast, and [[Tiritiri Matangi Island]] and [[Motutapu Island]] in the Hauraki Gulf. The Department of Conservation also runs a captive breeding and rearing programme at the Burwood Breeding Centre near Te Anau which has up to 25 breeding pairs.<ref name="hub" /> Chicks are reared with minimal human contact. The offspring of the captive birds are used for new island releases and to add to the wild population in the Murchison Mountains. The Department of Conservation also manages wild takahē nests to boost the birds' recovery.

An important management development has been the stringent control of deer in the Murchison Mountains and other takahē areas of Fiordland National Park. Following the introduction of deer hunting by helicopter, deer numbers have decreased dramatically and alpine vegetation is now recovering from years of heavy browsing. This improvement in its habitat has helped to increase takahē breeding success and survival. As of 2009, ongoing research aims to measure the impact of attacks by stoats and thus decide whether stoats are a significant problem requiring management.<ref name="RecoveryPlan" />

== Population ==
One of the original long-term goals was to establish a self-sustaining population of well over 500 South Island takahē. The population stood at 263 at the beginning of 2013. In 2016 the population rose to 306 takahē.<ref>{{cite web|title=Takahē population crosses 300 milestone|publisher=Department of Conservation|url=http://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2016/takahe-population-crosses-300-milestone/|access-date=2017-03-28|date=2016-12-02|language=en|archive-date=28 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170328105602/http://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2016/takahe-population-crosses-300-milestone/|url-status=live}}</ref> In 2017 the population rose to 347—a 13 percent increase from the last year.<ref name="auto"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/the-country/news/article.cfm?c_id=16&objectid=11944345|title=Takahe numbers rise|work=[[New Zealand Herald]] |date=15 November 2017|access-date=10 March 2019|archive-date=10 December 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191210004657/https://www.nzherald.co.nz/the-country/news/article.cfm?c_id=16&objectid=11944345|url-status=live}}</ref> In 2019, it increased to 418.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2019/takahe-population-flying-high/|title=Takahē population flying high|website=[[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]] |date=4 October 2019|access-date=7 June 2021|archive-date=23 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210923094802/https://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2019/takahe-population-flying-high/|url-status=live}}</ref> As of 2023, the population is around 500.<ref name="McClure2023">{{cite news |last1=McClure |first1=Tess |title=Prehistoric bird once thought extinct returns to New Zealand wild |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2023/aug/29/prehistoric-bird-once-thought-extinct-returns-to-new-zealand-wild |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=28 August 2023 |access-date=30 August 2023 |archive-date=15 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715101431/https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2023/aug/29/prehistoric-bird-once-thought-extinct-returns-to-new-zealand-wild |url-status=live }}</ref>

zh.extract:
巨水鸡（学名：Porphyrio hochstetteri，英文、毛利語：takahē，台湾作南秧雞），又名南島青水雞、南岛秧鸡、泰卡雞，是紫水雞屬的一種鳥類，分布于包括澳大利亚、紐西兰，塔斯马尼亚及其附近的岛屿。曾於1898年被認為經已滅絕，後於1948年再次被發現。


特徵

巨水鸡身材壮硕行动笨拙，红腿，黄嘴的根部有鲜红的瘤，翅膀蓝紫色，不能飞行。身长为63厘米（25英寸）左右，站立時高約50厘米（20英寸），重约为3公斤，很强壮，有着坚硬的鸟喙和强壮的腿。鼻沟浅而宽，鼻孔小而圆，在鼻沟前部下方，额甲宽大，后缘呈截形。翅圆形，第2枚、第3枚和第4枚初级飞羽最长，并几乎等长；第1枚和第6枚或第7枚初级飞羽等长。跗蹠和趾都长而有力；能用脚趾抓住和操纵食物，两性同型，体羽为紫色或蓝色，尾下覆羽白色。它们的腿又细又长，其中腿高占身高的一半以上，非常适于在草丛行走。


雌雄差异
雄性全身覆盖着漂亮的紫色羽毛，嘴和头的顶部及腿为朱红色。雌性和雄性的颜色截然不同，全身为灰褐色。


习性，繁殖

一般独居，栖息于新西兰南岛的高山草地环境中，脚上虽然没有蹼，但也会游泳、潜水。它们生活在杂草繁茂的地方，有时也会到较宽阔的水面附近去寻找食物。其食物以植物为主，也食一些螺类和水栖昆虫。繁殖季节雌雄同居，由雄鸟造巢。雌鸟在巢造好后便可产下5-10枚卵，夫妻双方轮流孵化3周后，雏鸡就会出壳。


保育现状
在新西兰的保护区内受严格保育措施，数量正在回升，由2013年的263隻升至2019年共418隻。截至2023年，全紐西蘭約有500隻南秧雞，每年以8%的速度成長。2023年，紐西蘭政府與當地的纳塔胡部落合作，放歸了9對南秧雞。
